symptoms.symptom_name: Gradual, painless, unilateral scrotal swelling over six months

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A slowly enlarging painless scrotal swelling is the classic hydrocele history.
A painless mass does not exclude a tumour; a tumour can be painless and can coexist with a hydrocele.

symptoms.symptom_name: Heaviness and dragging sensation worse on standing and heavy lifting

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Mechanical heaviness from the fluid mass, worse with gravity, is typical of a large hydrocele.
Worsening on standing and straining is shared with a hernia; not discriminating without examination.

symptoms.symptom_name: Painless swelling with normal overlying skin colour, no redness or warmth

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of pain, erythema and warmth argues strongly against an acute infective or inflammatory cause.
A painless swelling with normal skin fits a benign fluid collection.

symptoms.symptom_name: No dysuria, fever, chills or trauma

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: No urinary or systemic infective features lowers the probability of an infective scrotal cause.

symptoms.symptom_name: No abdominal pain, vomiting or bowel change (strangulated-hernia screen negative)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of obstructive/systemic features reduces the probability of strangulation but does not exclude a reducible hernia without examination.

symptoms.symptom_name: Smooth, tense, cystic scrotal mass with the testis not palpable separately, positioned anterior to the testis

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A tense cystic mass anterior to and enveloping the testis is characteristic of a hydrocele; sealed clinician exam finding unavailable to telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Brilliant transillumination of the mass (glows uniformly red to a focused light)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Brilliant transillumination indicates clear fluid rather than solid tissue; a clinician-elicited and clinician-interpreted sign the AI cannot obtain and must not simulate.
A solid tumour does not transilluminate; however, transillumination does not fully exclude a coexisting tumour, hence ultrasound remains required.

symptoms.symptom_name: No variation of the swelling with Valsalva; able to get above the swelling at the external inguinal ring; normal bowel sounds

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Getting above the swelling with no Valsalva variation and normal bowel sounds indicates a non-communicating scrotal collection rather than a hernia; sealed clinician exam finding.

symptoms.symptom_name: Anechoic fluid collection surrounding the anterolateral testis without solid septations, and a normal underlying testis, on scrotal ultrasound

investigation_details.expected_result: Anechoic fluid collection without solid septations surrounding a normal testis, confirming a simple hydrocele and excluding a solid tumour
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: An underlying testicular tumour hidden by the hydrocele would not be excluded.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: An anechoic fluid collection around a normal testis confirms a simple hydrocele; imaging never available in real time via telehealth.
Ultrasound demonstrating a normal underlying testis is the definitive exclusion of an occult solid tumour — the key sealed investigation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Characterises the scrotal mass, confirms a simple hydrocele and excludes an underlying testicular tumour — central to the in-person pathway the AI arranges, and something the AI cannot itself perform.
Palpation, transillumination and getting above the swelling separate a hydrocele from a hernia and a solid tumour; not obtainable over telehealth.
Considered only if ultrasound raises suspicion of a testicular tumour; not a first-line telehealth action and requires venous bloods the AI cannot obtain.
A hydrocele is a non-infective fluid collection; antibiotics are not indicated and the AI must not prescribe them remotely.
